concepts.name: Node.js
concepts.definition: Node.js is a JavaScript runtime that runs JavaScript outside a browser.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Node.js lets the same language used in a web page also run servers, command-line tools, and build scripts.
concepts.why: It is often chosen when a project wants JavaScript or TypeScript on the server and in developer tooling.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for JavaScript-based servers, automation, CLIs, and tools that need the npm ecosystem.
pitfalls: Do not block the event loop with slow CPU work.
Keep secrets in environment variables, not source files.

concepts.name: JavaScript
concepts.definition: JavaScript is a programming language used in browsers and in runtimes such as Node.js.
concepts.simpleDefinition: JavaScript tells a page or program what to do when data changes or a user takes an action.
concepts.why: It is used to make interfaces interactive and to implement server or tooling behavior in the JavaScript ecosystem.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a project runs in the browser or in a JavaScript runtime.
pitfalls: Understand asynchronous code before mixing promises and callbacks.
Validate data that comes from users or networks.

concepts.name: TypeScript
concepts.definition: TypeScript adds static type checking to JavaScript.
concepts.simpleDefinition: TypeScript lets you describe what shape data should have before the program runs.
concepts.why: Projects use it to catch mismatched values and unclear interfaces earlier while keeping JavaScript-compatible output.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a JavaScript codebase is large enough that data shapes and refactors need explicit checks.
pitfalls: Types disappear at runtime, so validate untrusted input too.
Avoid using any when a clearer type can be expressed.

concepts.name: SQL
concepts.definition: SQL is a language for defining, querying, and changing data in relational databases.
concepts.simpleDefinition: SQL is how an application asks a relational database for rows or tells it to save changes.
concepts.why: It is used when information has related tables, needs durable storage, and must be queried reliably.
uses: Write rows with INSERT or UPDATE
concepts.whenToUse: Use it with relational data such as users, orders, permissions, and reporting records.
pitfalls: Use parameterized queries to avoid injection.
Design indexes for the queries a product actually runs.

concepts.name: PostgreSQL
concepts.definition: PostgreSQL is an open-source relational database that uses SQL.
concepts.simpleDefinition: PostgreSQL is a durable place to keep related application data after a user closes the app.
concepts.why: Projects use it when they need transactions, relationships, constraints, and flexible SQL queries.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for durable relational data, especially when consistency and querying matter.
pitfalls: Keep credentials out of source control.
Use migrations so schema changes are repeatable.

concepts.name: React
concepts.definition: React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces from components.
concepts.simpleDefinition: React splits a screen into reusable pieces that update when their data changes.
concepts.why: Projects use it to organize interactive screens into components with clear props and state.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a web interface has interactive or reusable views.
pitfalls: Keep state close to where it is used.
Do not put side effects directly in rendering code.

concepts.name: Next.js
concepts.definition: Next.js is a React framework for building web applications with routing, rendering, and server capabilities.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Next.js gives a React project a standard way to organize pages, routes, and server-side work.
concepts.why: Projects use it to combine a React frontend with file-based routing and server-side capabilities in one framework.
uses: Render on the server or client
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for React web apps that benefit from integrated routing, rendering, and deployment conventions.
pitfalls: Learn which code runs on the server versus the browser.
Do not expose secrets to client-side code.

concepts.name: API
concepts.definition: An API is a defined interface through which one piece of software requests work or data from another.
concepts.simpleDefinition: An API is a contract that tells one part of a system how to ask another part for something.
concepts.why: Projects use APIs to separate a user-facing client from server logic, data access, or external services.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when separate parts of a system need a clear communication contract.
alternatives: Direct function calls in one process
pitfalls: Validate input at the boundary.
Return clear status codes and avoid leaking secrets in errors.

concepts.name: REST API
concepts.definition: A REST-style API uses HTTP methods and resource-oriented URLs to expose application operations.
concepts.simpleDefinition: A REST API gives a consistent web address and method for reading or changing a resource.
concepts.why: It is used because browsers, mobile apps, and services already understand HTTP conventions.
uses: PATCH or DELETE an existing resource
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for conventional web services where clear HTTP resource operations are a good fit.
pitfalls: Do not treat REST as a guarantee of good design.
Document request and response shapes.

concepts.name: Express
concepts.definition: Express is a Node.js web framework for defining routes and middleware.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Express lets a Node.js app receive web requests and decide what code responds.
concepts.why: It is used to build lightweight HTTP APIs and web servers with explicit route and middleware code.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a Node.js project needs a small and flexible HTTP server.
pitfalls: Handle errors centrally.
Validate untrusted request bodies before using them.

concepts.name: FastAPI
concepts.definition: FastAPI is a Python framework for building HTTP APIs using type hints and declared routes.
concepts.simpleDefinition: FastAPI lets a Python app turn functions into documented web endpoints.
concepts.why: It is often chosen when Python application logic needs a typed, documented HTTP interface.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when Python services need an HTTP API with validation and type-hint support.
pitfalls: Keep blocking work out of async routes.
Treat generated documentation as a contract that still needs tests.

concepts.name: Python
concepts.definition: Python is a general-purpose programming language used for applications, automation, data work, and services.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Python is a readable programming language often used to turn ideas, data, or AI workflows into working programs.
concepts.why: Projects use it for its approachable syntax and broad ecosystem for web services, automation, and data tooling.

syntax.code:
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f'Hello, {name}'

concepts.whenToUse: Use it for automation, backend services, data analysis, or AI-related workflows.
pitfalls: Use isolated environments for dependencies.
Add type hints and tests as projects grow.

concepts.name: Database
concepts.definition: A database stores data so it can be retrieved and changed beyond one temporary program run.
concepts.simpleDefinition: A database is the product's memory after the browser tab or server process is gone.
concepts.why: Projects use one when users, settings, records, or history need to survive between requests.

syntax.code:
INSERT INTO visits (user_id, created_at)
VALUES ($1, NOW());

concepts.whenToUse: Use it when important data must remain available after a request or restart.
alternatives: Flat files for tiny local tools
pitfalls: Do not expose database credentials to clients.
Design backups and migrations before production.

concepts.name: ORM
concepts.definition: An ORM maps application objects or models to rows in a relational database.
concepts.simpleDefinition: An ORM lets application code work with database records using program objects while it produces database queries underneath.
concepts.why: It is used to centralize data models, reduce repeated query boilerplate, and make migrations easier to manage.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a relational database is part of an application and the team benefits from model-level tooling.
pitfalls: Learn the SQL generated for important queries.
Do not assume an ORM removes the need for indexes or transactions.

concepts.name: Prisma
concepts.definition: Prisma is an ORM and database toolkit for JavaScript and TypeScript applications.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Prisma gives a TypeScript project a schema and generated client for talking to a database.
concepts.why: It is used to keep database models, migrations, and application data access in one typed workflow.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a Node.js or TypeScript project wants model definitions and typed database access.
pitfalls: Review generated migrations before applying them.
Keep schema changes compatible with deployed data.

concepts.name: Supabase
concepts.definition: Supabase provides hosted services around a PostgreSQL database, including authentication and client libraries.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Supabase gives a project a PostgreSQL database plus ready-made services such as authentication.
concepts.why: Projects use it to add a managed database and common backend services without building every service from zero.
uses: Call database APIs from a server or controlled client
concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a project needs PostgreSQL and managed backend features with a quick setup path.
pitfalls: Use row-level security deliberately.
Keep service-role keys on trusted servers only.

concepts.name: Docker
concepts.definition: Docker packages software and its dependencies into portable containers.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Docker helps a project run the same way on different computers by packaging its environment.
concepts.why: Projects use it to make development, testing, and deployment environments more repeatable.

syntax.code:
FROM node:20
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci

concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a project has services or dependencies that need a repeatable environment.
pitfalls: Do not bake secrets into images.
Keep images small and pin important base-image versions.

concepts.name: Git
concepts.definition: Git is a distributed version-control system for tracking changes in files.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Git is the time machine for a project: it records changes, branches experiments, and lets people collaborate.
concepts.why: Projects use it to review work, collaborate safely, and recover or compare changes over time.

syntax.code:
git switch -c feature/profile
git add .
git commit -m "Add profile flow"

concepts.whenToUse: Use it for nearly every software project, including local experiments worth preserving.
pitfalls: Never commit secrets.
Make focused commits that explain one change.

concepts.name: GitHub
concepts.definition: GitHub hosts Git repositories and collaboration tools such as pull requests, issues, and automation.
concepts.simpleDefinition: GitHub is where a Git project can be shared, reviewed, and improved with other people.
concepts.why: Projects use it to publish code, collaborate through reviews, and run automation around changes.

syntax.code:
git remote add origin https://github.com/owner/repository.git
git push -u origin main

concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a Git project needs a hosted collaboration space or public distribution.
pitfalls: Review what is staged before pushing.
Protect main branches and keep tokens private.

concepts.name: Middleware
concepts.definition: Middleware is code that runs between receiving a request and reaching the main application logic.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Middleware is a checkpoint in a request's journey that can inspect, change, redirect, or reject it.
concepts.why: Projects use it for repeated cross-cutting checks such as authentication, logging, validation, or redirects.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for a rule that should apply before multiple routes or handlers.
pitfalls: Keep middleware small and ordered deliberately.
Do not put slow unrelated work on every request.

concepts.name: Authentication
concepts.definition: Authentication verifies who a user or system is.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Authentication answers: who is making this request?
concepts.why: Projects use it to connect actions and data to an identity before allowing access.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it whenever a product has private accounts, user-owned data, or permissions.
pitfalls: Authentication is not authorization.
Never store plain-text passwords.

concepts.name: JWT
concepts.definition: A JSON Web Token is a compact token format that can carry signed claims between parties.
concepts.simpleDefinition: A JWT is a signed ID card a server can verify, but it must still be handled securely.
concepts.why: Projects use signed tokens to communicate identity or permissions across requests and services.

syntax.code: Authorization: Bearer <signed-token>

concepts.whenToUse: Use it only when a token-based session or service-to-service claim is appropriate.
pitfalls: A JWT is not encrypted by default.
Verify signature, expiry, issuer, and audience.

concepts.name: Tailwind CSS
concepts.definition: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first CSS framework that applies styles through composable class names.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Tailwind lets a developer style an element by combining small classes directly where the UI is written.
concepts.why: Projects use it to build consistent layouts and visual states without inventing a new CSS name for every small rule.

syntax.code:
<button class="rounded bg-emerald-700 px-3 py-2 text-white">
  Save
</button>

concepts.whenToUse: Use it when a team wants utility-based styling and a shared design-token vocabulary.
pitfalls: Keep repeated visual patterns in reusable components.
Do not mistake utility classes for accessible component behavior.

concepts.name: HTML
concepts.definition: HTML is the markup language used to structure web content and meaning.
concepts.simpleDefinition: HTML gives a page its meaningful building blocks such as headings, buttons, forms, and links.
concepts.why: Projects use it so browsers and assistive technologies can understand the structure of a screen.

syntax.code:
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input id="email" type="email" />

concepts.whenToUse: Use it for every browser-facing interface, including JSX which produces HTML-like output.
pitfalls: Use semantic elements before generic divs.
Pair inputs with visible labels.

concepts.name: CSS
concepts.definition: CSS controls the presentation, layout, and responsive appearance of HTML content.
concepts.simpleDefinition: CSS decides how a page looks and adapts across screen sizes.
concepts.why: Projects use it to separate visual presentation from the structure and behavior of a page.
concepts.whenToUse: Use it for browser layout and visual styling.
pitfalls: Test narrow and wide viewports.
Preserve visible focus states.

concepts.name: npm
concepts.definition: npm is the package manager and registry ecosystem commonly used with Node.js projects.
concepts.simpleDefinition: npm downloads JavaScript packages and runs project scripts described in package.json.
concepts.why: Projects use it to share dependencies, lock versions, and standardize common commands.

syntax.code:
npm install
npm run test

concepts.whenToUse: Use it in Node.js and JavaScript projects that use package.json.
pitfalls: Review new dependencies before installing them.
Do not edit lockfiles casually.

concepts.name: Ollama
concepts.definition: Ollama is a tool for downloading and running supported language models on a local machine.
concepts.simpleDefinition: Ollama lets an app ask an AI model on your own computer instead of sending the prompt to a cloud API.
concepts.why: Projects use it when local control, privacy, offline availability, or model experimentation matter.
uses: Experiment without a cloud API key

syntax.code:
ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b
ollama run qwen2.5-coder:7b

concepts.whenToUse: Use it when the machine has enough resources and local inference is preferable to a hosted model.
pitfalls: Models can require significant memory and disk space.
Local inference quality and speed depend on the machine and model.

concepts.name: Jupyter Notebook
concepts.definition: A Jupyter Notebook is a document containing executable code cells, text, and outputs.
concepts.simpleDefinition: A notebook is a step-by-step working document where code and explanation live together.
concepts.why: Projects use notebooks for exploration, data work, teaching, and reproducible demonstrations.

syntax.code:
# Cell 1
name = 'Rishi'
print(f'Hello, {name}')

concepts.whenToUse: Use it for explorations and tutorials; move stable production logic into modules when reuse and testing matter.
pitfalls: Cell execution order can hide state.
Restart and run all cells before trusting a result.
